examples.title: Recursive Preorder Traversal
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Preorder(node)
BEGIN
    IF node = NULL THEN
        RETURN
    ENDIF
    VISIT(node)          // Process current node
    Preorder(node.left)  // Traverse left subtree
    Preorder(node.right) // Traverse right subtree
END
examples.explanation: Preorder visits each node before its subtrees. First the root is processed, then recursively the entire left subtree, then the right subtree. For the example tree, output: A-B-D-E-C-F.

examples.title: Python Implementation of Inorder Traversal
examples.description: Both recursive and iterative inorder traversal implementations
examples.code:
# Recursive Inorder
def inorder_recursive(root):
    if root:
        inorder_recursive(root.left)
        print(root.val)  # Visit node
        inorder_recursive(root.right)

# Iterative Inorder using Stack
def inorder_iterative(root):
    stack = []
    current = root
    while current or stack:
        # Go to leftmost node
        while current:
            stack.append(current)
            current = current.left
        # Process node
        current = stack.pop()
        print(current.val)
        # Move to right subtree
        current = current.right
examples.explanation: Recursive version uses call stack implicitly. Iterative version uses explicit stack to simulate recursion: push all left nodes, pop and visit, then move right. Output for BST gives sorted order.

examples.title: C Implementation of Level Order Traversal
examples.explanation: Level order uses a queue (FIFO). Root is enqueued first, then processed and removed while its children are added. This ensures all nodes at depth d are processed before depth d+1. Output: A-B-C-D-E-F for the example tree.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is max width

examples.title: Java Postorder Traversal Implementation
examples.description: Postorder (Left-Right-Node) traversal showing both recursive and iterative approaches
examples.explanation: Postorder visits children before parent. Recursive version naturally visits left, right, then node. Iterative uses two stacks: first stack reverses order, second stack produces postorder. Output: D-E-B-F-C-A for example tree. Useful for tree deletion.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(h) for recursive, O(n) for iterative with two stacks
